primary_diagnosis.name: Deep full-thickness laceration of the left volar forearm requiring primary closure

pathophysiology_summary: A sharp incised wound from a chef's knife has produced a full-thickness cutaneous defect of the volar forearm, dividing epidermis, dermis and subcutaneous fat down to the superficial flexor fascia. Cleanly incised, widely gaping margins will not appose spontaneously, so the wound requires primary closure (suturing) to restore the dermal barrier, achieve haemostasis, minimise scarring and reduce infection risk. Bleeding is from divided subcutaneous/superficial venous channels (dark, steady ooze controllable by pressure) rather than a divided artery (which would spurt bright-red and pulsatile). The critical clinical work is excluding deep structural injury — a divided flexor tendon, a lacerated nerve, or an arterial injury — because these change the wound from a same-day closure into an urgent surgical problem. Reassuring gross motor function, intact sensation, controllable venous bleeding and (on in-person exam) palpable pulses, intact two-point discrimination and intact tendon sheaths place this as an uncomplicated laceration for primary closure within the suturing window. A contaminated (kitchen) wound with a last tetanus booster >10 years ago mandates tetanus prophylaxis.

differential.evidence_basis: Sharp incised, widely gaping wound with visible subcutaneous fat and active bleeding — a full-thickness defect that will not close on its own.

differential.diagnosis: Arterial laceration / traumatic arterial injury of the forearm
differential.evidence_basis: Any deep forearm laceration can divide the radial or ulnar artery; must be actively excluded before treating as a simple cut.
A volar forearm laceration overlies the flexor tendons; a divided tendon needs surgical repair and is easily missed.
Deep volar wounds can transect a nerve; altered sensation or two-point discrimination must be checked.
Excluded by the widely gaping margins and visible subcutaneous fat, which define a full-thickness defect, not a superficial graze.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At the opening the priority is to hold the mimics (arterial, tendon, nerve injury) open while confirming the wound needs in-person closure — not to close down prematurely on 'just a cut'.

differential.diagnosis: Uncomplicated full-thickness laceration for primary closure
differential.evidence_basis: Controllable dark venous ooze, normal gross finger/wrist movement and intact sensation reduce the likelihood of arterial, tendon and nerve injury.

differential.diagnosis: Arterial laceration / traumatic arterial injury of the forearm
differential.evidence_basis: Made less likely by dark non-pulsatile bleeding that responds to pressure, but only formal in-person pulse/perfusion assessment excludes it fully.
Made less likely by full active finger and wrist flexion, but partial tendon lacerations can retain movement — direct wound exploration is required to exclude.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Reassuring history downgrades but does not eliminate the surgical mimics; definitive exclusion needs in-person exploration, which telehealth cannot provide.

differential.diagnosis: Uncomplicated full-thickness volar forearm laceration for same-day primary closure, tetanus prophylaxis indicated
differential.evidence_basis: In-person exploration confirms exposed flexor fascia with intact tendon sheaths, palpable radial/ulnar pulses, intact two-point discrimination and full active range of motion — no deep structural injury; suitable for irrigation and layered primary closure within the golden window.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The 'final' picture reflects the in-person findings sealed here; the telehealth AI Doctor cannot reach this certainty and must arrange the in-person assessment that does.

red_flags.description: Active haemorrhage requiring sustained direct pressure to control
red_flags.clinical_significance: Ongoing blood loss; if pressure fails to control it or it soaks through, escalate emergently.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Continued blood loss and, if a vessel is involved, haemodynamic compromise.

red_flags.description: Signs of arterial injury — bright-red pulsatile spurting, expanding haematoma, cold/pale hand or absent pulses
red_flags.clinical_significance: Arterial laceration needs urgent surgical control; screened negative here (dark non-pulsatile ooze, warm hand, normal movement).
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Uncontrolled arterial haemorrhage or limb ischaemia.

red_flags.description: Flexor tendon or motor injury — inability to flex a finger or the wrist against resistance
red_flags.clinical_significance: A divided flexor tendon requires surgical repair and specialist referral; a missed tendon injury causes permanent functional loss.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Permanent loss of finger/wrist flexion if a divided tendon is not repaired.

red_flags.description: Peripheral nerve injury — numbness, tingling or loss of two-point discrimination distal to the wound
red_flags.clinical_significance: A transected nerve needs microsurgical repair; sensory loss is easily overlooked when attention is on the bleeding.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Permanent sensory loss or neuroma if a divided nerve is not repaired.

red_flags.description: Tetanus-prone wound with last tetanus-containing vaccine >10 years ago
red_flags.clinical_significance: A contaminated wound with lapsed tetanus cover requires a tetanus booster (dTpa) at the in-person visit; immunoglobulin only if the primary course is uncertain.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Risk of tetanus from a contaminated wound if prophylaxis is omitted.

red_flags.description: Closure window — a cleanly incised wound is best sutured within the golden window (hours), delay increases infection and scarring
red_flags.clinical_significance: Time-critical for optimal primary closure; the wound should be assessed and closed in person the same day, not managed at home.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed suturing window forces delayed or secondary closure with worse scarring and higher infection risk.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating a gaping full-thickness laceration as a trivial cut and advising home dressing/steri-strips, missing the need for in-person exploration and suturing within the golden window.
Focusing on the bleeding and failing to actively screen for arterial, tendon and nerve injury — the findings that separate a same-day closure from an urgent surgical referral.

Attempting to talk the patient through self-closure (glue/tape/steri-strips) of a wide full-thickness wound, or instructing self-injection of anaesthetic — procedures that require an in-person clinician.
Arranging closure but omitting the tetanus status check; a contaminated wound with a >10-year gap needs a booster.
Prescribing routine oral antibiotics for a clean laceration that will be promptly irrigated and closed — not indicated and an error of commission.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Direct wound exploration to confirm intact tendon sheaths and depth
Palpation of radial and ulnar pulses and capillary refill
Whether the wound base harbours foreign material after irrigation
Objective vital signs (BP, HR, temperature) without a home device
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Arrange urgent same-day in-person wound care (urgent care clinic, GP with minor-procedure capability, or ED) for exploration, irrigation, primary closure and tetanus prophylaxis within the suturing window; instruct firm continuous direct pressure and elevation in the meantime; escalate to emergency care if bleeding becomes uncontrollable or a neurovascular deficit appears.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The main risk is UNDER-action — reassuring the patient into home management, or missing an occult tendon/nerve/arterial injury that only in-person examination excludes.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: For a tetanus-prone wound, give a tetanus-containing vaccine if >5 years (tetanus-prone) since the last dose; add tetanus immunoglobulin if the primary course is incomplete or uncertain.

Cleanly incised lacerations are best closed by primary intention within the recommended window after thorough irrigation; routine antibiotic prophylaxis is not indicated for clean, promptly closed wounds.
